title: Spirit Airlines Collapses as Iran Tensions Rattle Markets; Powell Faces Historic Dissent

blocks.content: The collapse of Spirit Airlines after a failed White House rescue deal has sent shockwaves through airline stocks, while escalating U.S.-Iran tensions and a historic Federal Reserve split are compounding investor anxiety heading into a pivotal week.

Spirit Airlines officially ceased operations Saturday after the last-ditch effort to secure a federal bailout collapsed, marking the largest airline bankruptcy in recent memory. The carrier's immediate shutdown leaves thousands of travelers stranded and employees facing abrupt layoffs, while competing airlines are scrambling to absorb displaced passengers and available aircraft. For investors, the implosion underscores the structural challenges facing low-cost carriers in a high-inflation environment and raises questions about government willingness to intervene in future airline distress. Meanwhile, President Trump's threat to resume military strikes against Iran-coupled with a naval blockade of Iranian ports-has pushed crude oil prices higher and revived safe-haven demand for Treasuries.
• S&P 500: Fell 1.2% as Spirit's collapse and Iran headlines outweighed strong Berkshire earnings
• Nasdaq: Dropped 1.5% as investors rotated away from growth names after Fed dissent

• Berkshire Hathaway: Warren Buffett's conglomerate revealed a record $397 billion cash hoard with operating profits more than doubling, signaling the Oracle of Omaha sees few attractive opportunities at current valuations

• Japan Currency Defense: Tokyo deployed an estimated $34.5 billion to prop up the yen, triggering the currency's sharpest single-day rally and signaling authorities will not tolerate speculative moves

• Fed Faction Dissent: Four FOMC members broke ranks with Chair Powell's rate guidance in an unprecedented show of internal disagreement, raising questions about the central bank's independence and path forward
• UAE Oil Exit: The United Arab Emirates formally departed OPEC, citing that its $1.7 trillion sovereign wealth fund now benefits more from lower oil prices than production quotas
Traders will scrutinize the Bank of England's rate decision and accompanying commentary for signals on global monetary policy divergence.
